questions.question: Which property sets the background color of an element?
questions.explanation: background-color sets the background color. 'color' sets text color.

questions.question: What does background-repeat: no-repeat do?
options: Repeats the image in both directions
questions.explanation: no-repeat shows the background image only once without tiling.

questions.question: Which value for background-size ensures the image covers the entire element?
questions.explanation: cover scales the image to cover the entire element, potentially cropping parts of the image.

questions.question: What is the correct syntax for a linear gradient?
questions.explanation: linear-gradient(direction, color1, color2, ...) is the correct syntax.

questions.question: How do you set multiple background images?
questions.explanation: Separate multiple backgrounds with commas in the background shorthand or background-image property.

questions.question: What does background-attachment: fixed do?
options: Keeps the background fixed relative to the viewport during scroll
Makes the background scroll faster than content
Locks the background image in one position
questions.explanation: background-attachment: fixed keeps the background stationary relative to the viewport while content scrolls over it.

questions.question: What is the default value of background-repeat?
questions.explanation: The default value for background-repeat is 'repeat', which tiles the image both horizontally and vertically.

questions.question: Which background-clip value creates a text-masked gradient effect?
questions.explanation: background-clip: text clips the background to the shape of the text, creating gradient text when combined with color: transparent.

questions.question: What is the difference between background-size: cover and contain?
options: cover fills the element fully (may crop); contain shows entire image (may leave gaps)
cover shows entire image; contain fills the element
cover works for images; contain works for gradients
questions.explanation: cover scales to fill the element completely (cropping if needed). contain scales so the whole image is visible (leaving gaps if aspect ratio differs).

questions.question: What is a conic gradient used for?
options: Creating pie charts and color wheels
questions.explanation: Conic gradients transition colors around a center point, making them ideal for pie charts, color wheels, and circular progress indicators.

questions.question: In the background shorthand, how are position and size separated?
questions.explanation: Position and size are paired as 'position / size' in the background shorthand. The slash between them is critical.

questions.question: Where does background-color appear in relation to background-image?
options: It doesn't appear when an image is set
questions.explanation: Background color paints behind any background images. It shows through transparent portions of images and acts as a fallback.

questions.question: What does background-position: 50% 50% do?
options: Places the image at 50px from left and 50px from top
Centers the image within the element
Places the image at 50% of image size
questions.explanation: 50% 50% aligns the image's center point with the element's center point, effectively centering the background image.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using a CSS gradient over an image file for simple patterns?
options: Gradients reduce HTTP requests and are GPU-accelerated
questions.explanation: CSS gradients are generated by the browser, eliminating HTTP requests and are often GPU-accelerated for better performance.

questions.question: How do you create a hard-stop gradient with no transition?
options: Set adjacent color stops at the same position
questions.explanation: A hard stop is created by placing two color stops at the same position, e.g., linear-gradient(to right, red 50%, blue 50%).

questions.question: What does background-origin control?
options: Where the background positioning area is measured from
The origin point of the image file
questions.explanation: background-origin defines the positioning area reference (border-box, padding-box, or content-box) for background images.

questions.question: Which of these is a valid way to create a semi-transparent overlay on a background image?
options: Layer a gradient with rgba over the image
questions.explanation: Multiple techniques work: pseudo-elements with opacity, gradient overlays with rgba, and background-blend-mode.

questions.question: What does background-repeat: space do?
options: Creates empty space around the image
Tiles the image with even spacing between tiles
Stretches the image to fill space
questions.explanation: space tiles the image and distributes even spacing between the tiles so they fit perfectly within the container.

questions.question: Why is it important to always set a background-color when using background-image?
options: It adds depth to the design
It acts as a fallback when images fail to load
It's required by the CSS specification
questions.explanation: A background-color fallback ensures the element has visual content even when the background image fails to load or loads slowly.

questions.question: For a responsive hero section with a background image, what combination is recommended?
options: Always use the same image at all sizes
Use different background images at different breakpoints via media queries
questions.explanation: Using different background images at different breakpoints ensures optimal image size and quality across devices.
